stories.headline: OPENAI REVENUE MISS TRIGGERS AI INFRASTRUCTURE ROUT — NVIDIA AND BROADCOM EACH DOWN 3-4%, NASDAQ -0.90%
stories.body: A WSJ report revealed OpenAI has missed internal user-growth and revenue projections, sparking a broad selloff across the AI supply chain: Nvidia, Broadcom, and AMD each fell 3-4%; Oracle (a $300B five-year OpenAI compute partner) dropped 3%+; neocloud CoreWeave shed 4%+; SoftBank — OpenAI's largest outside investor — sank ~10% in Tokyo. The S&P 500 fell 0.49% to 7,138 and the Russell 2000 dropped 1.15%, as investors rotated out of AI-exposed names and into defensive sectors.

stories.headline: WTI SURGES TO $101 — SEVENTH STRAIGHT GAIN — AS IRAN STRAIT TRAFFIC COLLAPSES TO NEAR ZERO
stories.body: WTI crude rose to $101/bbl and Brent touched $111.57, both hitting their highest levels since early April, as mutual US-Iran restrictions have effectively shut tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — which carries ~20% of global petroleum liquids daily. Supply anxiety is building a steep risk premium into the forward curve, with front-month backwardation widening on fears of prolonged disruption.

stories.headline: FED OPENS TWO-DAY MEETING — 100% ODDS OF HOLD AT 3.50-3.75%; WEDNESDAY PRESSER IS THE REAL EVENT
stories.body: The FOMC's April 28-29 meeting opens with CME FedWatch pricing a 100% probability of a third consecutive hold at 3.50-3.75%. The real market event is Wednesday's 2:30 PM ET Powell press conference — likely his final as chair — where investors will parse whether $101 WTI is treated as a transitory supply shock (keeping H2 2026 cuts alive) or a persistent inflation driver (pushing the first cut to 2027).

stories.headline: US-IRAN CEASEFIRE STALLS; HORMUZ CLOSURE THREATENS TO EMBED OIL SHOCK INTO CORE CPI
stories.body: Despite a ceasefire nominally holding since early April, mutual restrictions have choked the Strait of Hormuz to near-zero tanker traffic. With Brent at $111.57 and WTI at $101 — roughly $15/bbl above early-April levels — the energy pass-through to headline CPI is accelerating. Every $10/bbl move in crude adds an estimated 20-25 bps to US headline inflation within 60-90 days, meaning the current spike alone contributes ~35 bps of incremental upward pressure into the Fed's already-missed target.

stories.headline: IMF APRIL 2026 WEO: 'GLOBAL ECONOMY TESTED AGAIN' — TARIFFS AND ENERGY SHOCK COMPOUND INTO STAGFLATION RISK
stories.body: The IMF's April 2026 World Economic Outlook trimmed its global growth baseline as Trump tariffs — the largest US tax increase as a share of GDP since 1993, costing ~$1,500/US household this year — combine with the Iran energy shock. The Fund projects the US economy is persistently 0.1% smaller (~$30B/year) in the long run due to tariff drag, and warns core PCE normalization toward 2% is now contingent on oil reversing — a condition in serious doubt.

stories.headline: MICHIGAN CHAMPIONSHIP ROSTER FRAGMENTS AS NBA EARLY ENTRY WINDOW CLOSES — BIG TEN STARS DECLARE
stories.body: Three weeks after Michigan defeated UConn in the 2026 NCAA title game — the Wolverines dominated with a 91-73 semifinal win over Arizona before topping the Huskies in the final — multiple key contributors have declared for the NBA Draft as the late-April early entry deadline passes. Michigan's first championship since 1989 accelerated interest in its frontcourt and backcourt stars, with several projected as first-round talents in early mock drafts.

stories.headline: MAGIC PUSH PISTONS TO BRINK (3-1); THREE GAME 5S TONIGHT — CELTICS-76ERS, KNICKS-HAWKS, SPURS-BLAZERS
stories.body: Orlando defeated Detroit 94-88 in Game 4 — Desmond Bane led with 22 pts while the Pistons shot 37.8% from the field and missed 8 free throws — giving the Magic a commanding 3-1 lead. Tonight's slate features three elimination-adjacent Game 5s: Celtics vs. 76ers (7 ET, ESPN), Knicks vs. Hawks (8 ET, NBC), and Spurs vs. Blazers (9:30 ET, ESPN), where San Antonio can close out after their historic 38-point halftime-to-final swing in Game 4.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL DRAFT COMPLETE: SEAHAWKS TAP RB JADARIAN PRICE AT #32; UDFA MARKET MOVES FAST
stories.body: The 2026 NFL Draft concluded April 25 in Pittsburgh with IU QB Fernando Mendoza going #1 overall to Las Vegas. The Seahawks — picking last as defending Super Bowl champions — selected Notre Dame RB Jadarian Price at #32 to replace the departed Kenneth Walker III. Teams are now aggressively signing undrafted free agents: the Ravens inked QB Diego Pavia (Vanderbilt) to a three-year deal, and more than 200 UDFA agreements have been reported league-wide.

stories.headline: SPRING BALL CONCLUDES NATIONWIDE; UW QB DEMOND WILLIAMS JR. EMERGES STRONGER AFTER PORTAL SCARE
stories.body: Spring practices are wrapping up across Power Four programs as depth charts crystallize ahead of summer camp. At Washington, QB Demond Williams Jr. — who briefly entered the transfer portal in January before reversing course within 48 hours — has been praised by coaches for a markedly more vocal and intentional approach, and holds the QB1 role firmly. The 2026 Huskies open September 5 vs. Washington State under a new offensive coordinator after parting ways with Jimmie Dougherty.

sports.detail: Lost Game 4 to Magic on April 27. Tobias Harris 20 pts/6 reb; Duren 12 pts/8 reb but 4 TOs. Shot 37.8% from field, missed 8 FTs. Magic lead series 3-1; Pistons face elimination in Game 5.

Hammered at Target Field on April 27 — Twins' Kody Clemens homered and drove in 5 runs, Connor Prielipp earned his first MLB win. Record now 14-16. Tonight's game vs. Minnesota (7:40 PM CT) in progress.

Draft complete (April 23-25). Selected RB Jadarian Price (Notre Dame) at #32 overall to replace SB MVP Kenneth Walker III. Converted two picks into five on Day 3. Now in UDFA signing window; training camp opens July.

Spring practices wrapping up. QB Demond Williams Jr. back as QB1 after brief portal stint, described as more vocal and intentional. New OC hired after Dougherty departure. Season opener: Sept. 5 vs. Washington State.

Major roster overhaul underway. Added transfers Steele Venters (Gonzaga), LeJuan Watts (Texas Tech, Apr 25), Ryan Beasley and Parker Friedrichsen (San Francisco). First HS commit: Mount Si F Lattimore Ford. 10 of 15 spots filled.

watchlist: Fed rate decision Wednesday Apr 29, 2 PM ET — 100% hold at 3.50-3.75%; Powell presser 2:30 PM ET — watch 'transitory' vs. 'persistent' framing on oil/inflation
Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate Thursday Apr 30 — energy shock and tariff drag expected to weigh on real growth print
Core PCE Friday May 1 — Fed's preferred gauge; elevated print could extinguish remaining 2026 cut expectations entirely
Pistons-Magic Game 5 — Wednesday Apr 29, must-win for Detroit (down 3-1); elimination game
Mariners vs. Twins — series continues Apr 28-29 at Target Field; M's (14-16) need wins to stay in AL West conversation

deepDive.title: The AI Capex Flywheel: Why One OpenAI Revenue Miss Crashed the Entire Chip Sector
content: A single Wall Street Journal report — that OpenAI missed internal user-growth and revenue projections, with CFO Sarah Friar warning colleagues that slower growth could constrain future compute commitments — sent Nvidia, Broadcom, and AMD each down 3-4%, Oracle down 3%+, and neocloud CoreWeave down 4%+, all on a Tuesday with no macro catalyst. The Nasdaq closed at 24,663, off 0.9%. The cascade looks outsized for news about one private startup, but it exposes a specific structural vulnerability in how the entire AI infrastructure trade is priced: a chain of multi-year compute agreements whose unit economics depend on AI application revenue growing fast enough to service enormous upfront costs.

The core mechanism is the AI capex flywheel. OpenAI and peer AI labs commit to massive multi-year compute agreements — the OpenAI-Oracle partnership alone spans $300 billion over five years — premised on continued rapid revenue growth to justify that spend. Chip manufacturers (Nvidia, Broadcom) and infrastructure providers (Oracle, CoreWeave, Microsoft Azure) price their stocks on forward expectations of that demand. The chain is reflexive: AI lab revenue → renewed compute commitments → chip and infrastructure forward order books → forward earnings estimates for public vendors. When one lab's revenue growth decelerates, every upstream vendor's forward demand assumptions weaken simultaneously, which is why a private company missing internal targets can move billions in public-market cap.

What most coverage misses is that this is a comps problem, not an earnings problem. OpenAI has no quarterly report — there's no official earnings miss, only a trajectory shift. The strongest historical analogue is the 1999-2001 telecom buildout: carriers committed $400B+ in combined network capex premised on internet traffic doubling every 90 days. When growth decelerated from exponential to merely extraordinary, the entire supply chain — fiber, optical components, routers — repriced violently. Cisco lost 89% of its value. The 2026 question is structurally identical: is AI compute demand an S-curve still on its steep mid-slope, or a near-exponential already approaching an inflection where growth rates moderate even as absolute levels remain high?

Today's market action contains a key signal: the S&P 500 fell only 0.49% while chip-specific names fell 3-4% — a relative underperformance consistent with a sector re-rating, not a broad macro panic. Nvidia currently trades around 30x forward earnings, a multiple that embeds a specific compound growth rate assumption for data center revenue. If OpenAI's miss suggests AI revenue is running one or two quarters behind plan, that multiple is vulnerable to compression even without an earnings cut. The next hard data points are Nvidia's upcoming earnings print and Q2 capex guidance from the hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft, Google). If those reaffirm AI buildout plans, today is a single-data-point overreaction. If they soften, this is the beginning of a structural re-rating.
